one_line_takeaway: A comprehensive, up-to-date (180+ papers) survey that reorganizes LLM unlearning by pipeline phase -- training-time / post-training / inference-time -- and cross-cuts post-training methods by update rule vs parameter target, while cataloging 18 benchmarks and 10 memorization-metric categories and stressing that current unlearning is fragile against relearning, cross-lingual prompts, and quantization.
key_finding: The organizing insight is that unlearning interventions can be cleanly separated by pipeline phase, and within post-training the 'how to update' (SFT/RL objective) and 'which parameters' (full/localized/added-structure) decisions are orthogonal and independently combinable. On evaluation, the survey's synthesis surfaces that current metrics and definitions are inconsistent (some methods want the model to refuse, others to output a plausible wrong answer, others just to not emit the target), that MIA -- though common -- is ill-suited to LLMs because it needs computationally intractable shadow models, and that unlearning is fragile: relearning on sparse cues, cross-lingual prompting, and quantization can all restore erased knowledge.
core_contribution: A systematic survey of over 180 LLM-unlearning papers published since 2021. Its own contributions (not the surveyed methods) are threefold: (1) a novel taxonomy that organizes unlearning methods by the PHASE of intervention in the LLM pipeline -- training-time (Sec 3.1), post-training (Sec 3.2), and inference-time (Sec 3.3) -- and, within post-training, cross-cuts them along two orthogonal axes: how parameters are modified (SFT vs RL) and which parameters are targeted (full / localized / newly-inserted). The authors argue this usage-oriented framing is superior to prior objective- or intention-based taxonomies because it lets a single method (e.g. RMU) be analyzed separately for its loss design and its parameter-selection mechanism. (2) A multidimensional analysis of evaluation: a comparison of 18 benchmarks across content (real vs fictional), task format, and experiment paradigm (with/without fine-tuning), plus a division of knowledge-memorization metrics into 10 categories rather than a flat enumeration. (3) A structured discussion of open challenges (inconsistent definitions of memorization, cross-lingual and cross-data variability, real-world scaling) and future directions.
threat_model: The survey frames unlearning as a defensive/compliance technique against risks that LLMs create by memorizing training data. The threats it catalogs (as motivation, drawn from cited work, not the survey's own experiments) are: extraction of memorized personally identifiable information via privacy/data-extraction attacks ('right to be forgotten'), reproduction of copyrighted material, and elicitation of hazardous or harmful knowledge (e.g. bioweapon/cyber knowledge, jailbreaks). It also treats robustness adversarially: unlearning is considered successful only if erased knowledge resists recovery via input attacks (jailbreak prompts, GCG, AutoPrompt, PAIR), hidden-layer probing (Logit Lens, soft-prompt threats), relearning on a small data subset, and quantization -- all of which are shown in the literature to reactivate supposedly-unlearned knowledge.
limitations: As a survey it introduces no new method, benchmark, or empirical measurement, so all quantitative claims are inherited from cited work rather than independently verified. The authors explicitly scope out unlearning for classification/NLP tasks (focusing only on generative LLMs) and treat adjacent fields (knowledge editing, alignment, memorization/data-extraction) only briefly. They note the field's own gaps as limitations of the surveyed area: experiments are largely confined to sub-10B-parameter models and unlearn sets under 1B instances, no agreed definition of memorization exists, and no current method reliably handles sequential unlearning requests.

datasets_used: TOFU (fictional QA, 200x20 QA pairs, short-answer QA; most widely reused benchmark) [catalogued, not run by the survey]
WMDP (3,668 MCQA on hazardous bio/cyber/chem knowledge; real, no fine-tuning) [catalogued]
WHP / Who's Harry Potter (real, 3.1M tokens, 300 continuation + 30 cloze) [catalogued]
RWKU (200 celebrities, 3,268 cloze + 2,879 SAQA, no fine-tuning) [catalogued]

Other benchmarks tabled: CoTaEval, KnowUnDo, PISTOL, WPU, ConceptVectors, EDU-RELAT, ELUDe, FaithUn, LLM Surgery, Restor, RETURN, UNCD; plus adjacent-field sets CounterFact, PKU-SafeRLHF, SQuAD, ZsRE [catalogued]
General-utility benchmarks referenced: MMLU, ARC, GSM8K, MathQA, HellaSwag, PIQA, WinoGrande, Lambada, OpenBookQA, MT-Bench, TruthfulQA [catalogued]
quantitative_results: Surveys more than 180 LLM-unlearning papers published since 2021
Compares 18 existing unlearning benchmarks across task format, content, and experiment paradigm
Divides knowledge-memorization metrics into 10 categories (across output-based model-free/model-based, logit-based probability/rank, and MIA)
Categorizes methods into 3 intervention phases (training-time, post-training, inference-time) and post-training into 5 sub-areas (SFT, RL, localization, new-structure, composite)
Groups general-utility benchmarks into 7 classes (reasoning, math, commonsense, comprehension, universal knowledge, multi-turn, mimic-human)
Notes field-wide scaling ceiling: experiments largely limited to <10B-parameter models and unlearn sets <1B instances
baselines_compared: None of the survey's own -- it runs no experiments. It qualitatively contrasts representative surveyed methods (e.g. Gradient Ascent vs NPO/SimNPO; RMU vs Adaptive-RMU/LUNAR; standard LoRA vs FILA/LOKA; delta-UNLEARNING vs ULD) but reports no head-to-head numbers it generated.
